title: PRD: Item-first lifecycle traceability for PM work
problem_summary: PMs lack a single view that explains where one opportunity is in discovery, which artifacts exist, who it serves, which gates passed, and what downstream work is ready.
outcome_summary: A PM can inspect one item end to end through release readiness and review lifecycle snapshots without losing artifact lineage, gate confidence, or delivery context.
scope_summary: V6.0 extends the lifecycle trace from PRD handoff through story planning, acceptance readiness, and release readiness, adds concrete delivery, launch, outcome, and full-lifecycle snapshots, and keeps launch execution and outcome review explicitly deferred.
strategic_context_summary: The PRD should preserve the item-first challenger posture: improve lifecycle explanation and governance without claiming to replace every PM or execution system.
value_hypothesis: If ProductOS keeps one opportunity traceable across delivery artifacts and gates, PMs should be able to review status and readiness with much less manual reconstruction.
target_outcomes: A PM can inspect one item through release readiness without rebuilding the story.
Lifecycle snapshots remain believable because artifacts, gates, and deferred stages are explicit.

prioritization.priority_rationale: The PRD is the execution handoff that proves the lifecycle slice can move beyond discovery without losing the traceability boundary.
prioritization.reviewer_handoff: Design and engineering should review scope boundaries before story planning or acceptance automation expands the slice.

scope_boundaries: Stay focused on one item trace through release readiness.
Keep launch execution and outcome review explicitly deferred in this bounded slice.
out_of_scope: Broad launch automation or outcome review execution in V6.
Replacing incumbent planning and execution systems.
open_questions: Which later-stage artifacts should become mandatory if the lifecycle slice expands beyond release readiness?
handoff_risks: Story and acceptance work may drift if the PRD stops carrying the explicit deferred-stage boundary.
The release claim will overreach if the PRD implies complete lifecycle coverage beyond the current scope.
